sections.name: Subject Line (47 chars)
sections.content:
> Pre-K is coming, this my last email

Rationale: Observation hook per L1 subject line patterns (Layer-04 §3.15, 15-level-messaging-matrix §2.3). Under 50 characters. Names Pre-K deadline without pressure. "This my last email" signals respect for her timeline, no guilt, no "one more try" manipulation. Frame from S2 copy strategy §1.21: "Soft final invitation."

### Preview Text (92 chars)

> The door's open whenever you are. If that's today or never, you've got my respect either way.

Rationale: Complements (does not repeat) the subject line. Reinforces permission-not-to-buy. Uses L1-approved language pattern "whatever you choose" from 11-brand-voice-reference §7.1 universal approved phrases. 92 chars, under the 130 char max and close to the 90 char mobile preview sweet spot (GIULIANO-DEMANDS.md §11.2).

### From Name

> Lena from BrightKidCo

Rationale: Per GIULIANO-DEMANDS.md §16.3 Klaviyo Technical Setup. Single from-name throughout Phase 1 warming per §17.10. "Customer Support · Mom of two autistic sons" appears as profile subtitle below sign-off per Layer-09 §8.1.

sections.name: Body
sections.content:
---

Hi {first_name},

I want to be direct with you.

You signed up {X} weeks ago. Pre-K is coming, I know that deadline is real, and I know you've been thinking about it. I also know that if Body-Signal learning was going to click for your family, it probably would have by now.

It's okay if this wasn't the right time.

You didn't fail. The method wasn't built wrong for your kid, your kid's nervous system is on its own schedule, and no product, no matter how well-designed, can rush a body that isn't ready yet.

Here's the part I want you to remember:

You've already done the hardest thing, you showed up. You researched. You cared enough to read 10+ emails from a stranger who's been where you are. That's nothing. That's everything.

The signal-timing gap is real.

Your child may be verbal, smart, "almost trained", and still not feel when they need to go. That's not defiance. That's a signal that arrives milliseconds too late, every time. Interoception research from Nicholson et al. (2019) shows this a measurable neurological difference in autistic children, not a parenting failure, not a child refusing to cooperate.

The Body-Signal Learning Layer creates a 30-60 second sustained "uh-oh" sensation that bridges that gap. It gives a slow to pick up signals brain the feedback window it needs to finally register: *something happened.*

If you ever want to try it, the 3+3 Bundle is here.

$79.99. Free shipping. 60 days to test it by your own judgment. If your child shows any sign of body awareness, a pause, a look, a step toward the bathroom, you'll know the signal is working. If nothing changes, full refund. No questions. No hoops.

And if today isn't the day, that's a real answer.

The product will be here next month. Next year. Whenever you're ready. I will too.

This my last email in this series. I'm not going to keep filling your inbox. But my door, and the 60-day guarantee, is always open.

---

sections.name: CTA
sections.content:
> Try the 3+3 Bundle, 60 days, your judgment →
*That's $13.33 per pair with the bundle — less than half the single-pair price.*

**
Button Design: Full-width, Brand-Green (#039902), 48px min height (mobile tap-friendly). White text. Border-radius 12-16px per Design Rules (GIULIANO-DEMANDS.md §15.4). Hover state: darken to #028002.

Link: https://www.brightkidco.com/products/potty-training-underwear-for-sensory-sensitive-toddlers-sp (ASD PDP per locked URLs, GIULIANO-DEMANDS.md §16.4)

---

### Lena Sign-Off, LONG Variant (Winback)

Body (max 2-3 sentences):

I have two autistic sons. One trained at 6, the other at almost 8. I know what it's like when "later" keeps stretching into another year. But I also know what it's like to push too early.

Sign-Off Closing:

> With care,
>
> Lena
> Customer Support · Mom of two autistic sons

P.S. (max 2 lines):

P.S. If you ever want to talk it through, hit reply. You'll get me, not a bot. No sales pitch. Just another mom who's been there.

---
